column1: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target
column30:
Migrate the monitored infrastructure (Spring Boot, Kong, AI RCA pipeline, Ollama) from per-VM docker-compose to a single-node k3s cluster on EC2. The monitoring stack (Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Jaeger) stays on its own VM and reaches into the cluster via kubernetes_sd_configs and an in-cluster OTel Collector DaemonSet. This validates the architectural shift to declarative, reproducible deployment with K8s as a portable substrate while preserving the existing observability platform.

Goals:
- Single-node k3s cluster on EC2 (HA deferred to sprint 3)
- Three Helm charts: Spring Boot (in-house), Kong (upstream), AI stack (in-house)
- Image registry pipeline (ECR + GitHub Actions)
- Monitoring VM unchanged but reconfigured to scrape the cluster
- All 17 Grafana alert rules migrated to K8s label selectors
- End-to-end validation against the K8s target

Out of scope (sprint 3+):
- Multi-node HA / k3s embedded etcd
- ArgoCD/GitOps adoption
- Calico/Cilium for NetworkPolicy enforcement
- Longhorn for distributed storage
- OpenTelemetry Operator for auto-instrumentation
- Migrating the monitoring stack itself to K8s (deliberately rejected)

column1: E3-00: Team K8s onboarding — Phases 1-4 of the Kubernetes guide on k3d
column30:
As a developer working on the Kubernetes migration, I need hands-on familiarity with K8s primitives so that I can build Epic 3 stories without fighting conceptual misunderstandings.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Each contributor brings up a local k3d cluster: k3d cluster create learn --servers 1 --agents 2
- Walks through Phase 1 of the Kubernetes guide (deploy nginx, port-forward, watch self-healing)
- Walks through Phase 2 (ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, namespaces, Job, DaemonSet)
- Walks through Phase 3 (DNS resolution, Service vs Pod IPs, NetworkPolicy)
- Walks through Phase 4 (install kube-prometheus-stack via Helm, override values)
- Completes a 30-min pair walkthrough with at least one teammate to demonstrate understanding
- Reference: https://github.com/linalaaraich/monitoring-docs/blob/main/kubernetes-guide.html

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 1 day per contributor
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: ALL other E3 stories
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-01: Bootstrap k3s on EC2 via Ansible — xanmanning.k3s, pinned, Traefik disabled
column30:
As a platform engineer, I need a single-node k3s cluster running on EC2 so that the Helm-deployed workloads have somewhere to run.

Acceptance Criteria:
- xanmanning.k3s role added to requirements.yml, version v3.4.4 pinned
- New playbook playbooks/k3s.yml bootstraps k3s on a single host
- k3s_release_version pinned to a specific v1.30.x+k3s1 release
- Traefik disabled via k3s_server.disable: [traefik] (we use Kong)
- write-kubeconfig-mode: '0644' so kubeconfig is fetchable
- kubeconfig fetched back to control machine at artifacts/kubeconfig.yaml
- Server URL in fetched kubeconfig rewritten from 127.0.0.1 to the EC2 host IP
- New inventory group k3s_cluster wired into site.yml
- Verification: kubectl --kubeconfig=artifacts/kubeconfig.yaml get nodes returns 1 Ready node
- README updated with the new bootstrap step

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-03, E3-04, E3-05, E3-06, E3-07, E3-08, E3-09, E3-10
Blocked by: E3-00, SCRUM-63 (rescoped Terraform)
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-02: Container image pipeline — ECR + GitHub Actions for triage and 5 MCP servers
column30:
As a platform engineer, I need an image registry the cluster can pull from and a CI pipeline that builds and pushes images on commit so that Helm-deployed pods can run our first-party code.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ECR repositories created via Terraform: cires/triage-service, cires/mcp-prometheus, cires/mcp-loki, cires/mcp-jaeger, cires/mcp-drain3, cires/mcp-rca-history
- GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/build-images.yml builds and pushes on push to main and on tags
- Images tagged with both git short-SHA and semver tag
- Pull authentication from k3s to ECR via IRSA, OR via imagePullSecret created from ECR auth token (with refresh CronJob)
- Dockerfiles for triage service and 5 MCP servers extracted from current docker-compose builds
- Verification: docker pull <ecr-url>/cires/triage-service:<sha> works from a fresh machine
- All image references in Helm charts use pinned tags, never :latest

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-05 (AI stack chart cannot deploy without images in registry)
Blocked by: E3-00
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-03: Helm chart — Spring Boot backend (Deployment + Service + ConfigMap + probes)
column30:
As a developer, I need a Helm chart that deploys the Spring Boot backend into the k3s cluster so that the application is reachable from Kong and can talk to RDS.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New chart at charts/spring-boot/ with Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates/
- Deployment template with: 1+ replicas (configurable), resource requests + limits, liveness probe (/actuator/health/liveness), readiness probe (/actuator/health/readiness)
- Service template (ClusterIP, port 80 -> targetPort 8080)
- ConfigMap for Spring application properties (profile, log levels, etc.)
- Secret reference for RDS credentials (DB_USERNAME, DB_PASSWORD, DB_URL)
- ServiceAccount for the pod
- Image references pinned by tag (no :latest)
- values.yaml documents every override
- Installable via helm install spring-boot charts/spring-boot/ --namespace app --create-namespace -f values/spring-boot.yaml
- Pod reaches RDS successfully (verified via /actuator/health endpoint)
- Spring Boot logs include trace_id (OTel Java agent injected via init container or baked into image)

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-09, E3-10
Blocked by: E3-01
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-04: Helm chart — Kong via upstream chart, DB-less mode, kong.yml as ConfigMap
column30:
As a platform engineer, I need Kong running inside the cluster as the API gateway so that external traffic reaches the Spring Boot backend through the existing declarative routing config.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Upstream kong/kong Helm chart used, chart_version pinned (e.g. 2.38.0)
- DB-less mode enabled
- Existing kong.yml declarative config converted to a ConfigMap mounted into the Kong pod
- Upstream URLs in kong.yml rewritten from application_vm_ip:8080 to spring-boot.app.svc.cluster.local:80
- React frontend route still points to CloudFront (unchanged)
- OTel plugin enabled, sends spans to in-cluster OTel Collector via OTLP
- Prometheus plugin enabled, /metrics endpoint scraped by Prometheus K8s SD
- Service exposed externally via NodePort or LoadBalancer (decision documented)
- Verification: curl through Kong reaches Spring Boot, traces appear in Jaeger
- Decision documented: which Service type for Kong's external port and why

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-09, E3-10
Blocked by: E3-01, E3-03
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-05: Helm chart — AI stack (triage service + 5 MCP servers + Ollama)
column30:
As an SRE, I need the entire AI RCA pipeline deployed via a single Helm chart so that triage, MCP servers, and Ollama have a consistent lifecycle and can roll back together.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New chart at charts/ai-stack/ containing:
  - Triage service Deployment + Service (port 8090)
  - 5x MCP server Deployments + Services (Prometheus :8091, Loki :8092, Jaeger :8093, Drain3 :8094, RCA History :8095)
  - Ollama Deployment with PVC for model storage (20Gi minimum, local-path StorageClass)
  - GPU resource request on Ollama pod: resources.limits.nvidia.com/gpu: 1
  - Init Job or postStart hook that pulls the Ollama model on first deploy
  - Secret for SMTP credentials and Ollama config
  - All inter-service references use Service DNS (no localhost, no hardcoded IPs)
- All image references pinned to ECR tags from E3-02
- Resource requests + limits set per container
- Liveness + readiness probes per container
- Installable via helm install ai-stack charts/ai-stack/ --namespace ai --create-namespace -f values/ai-stack.yaml
- Verification: triage /health responds, MCP servers respond, Ollama returns model list

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-09, E3-10
Blocked by: E3-01, E3-02, E3-06
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-06: NVIDIA device plugin DaemonSet + Ollama GPU verification spike
column30:
As a platform engineer, I need to verify that GPU passthrough works on k3s so that Ollama can leverage the GPU for inference. This is a spike — plan for fiddly debugging.

Acceptance Criteria:
- NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed on the k3s host (via Ansible role nvidia_docker, repurposed)
- containerd configured to recognize the nvidia runtime (k3s --default-runtime=nvidia or RuntimeClass)
- NVIDIA device plugin DaemonSet deployed (nvidia/k8s-device-plugin v0.15.0 manifest)
- Test pod requesting nvidia.com/gpu: 1 runs successfully and nvidia-smi output is visible
- Troubleshooting guide added to monitoring-docs covering common failures (driver mismatch, runtime not configured, plugin pod CrashLoopBackOff)
- Decision documented: GPU on the same EC2 as everything else, or separate g4dn.xlarge worker node
- If a separate GPU node is chosen: node tainted, Ollama pod has matching toleration

Story Points: 5
Estimate: 1 day (spike, may extend to 1.5)
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-05 (Ollama in AI stack chart cannot run without this)
Blocked by: E3-01
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-07: In-cluster OTel Collector DaemonSet — log/trace forwarding to monitoring VM
column30:
As an observability engineer, I need an OTel Collector running on every K8s node so that container logs and traces are enriched with K8s metadata and forwarded to the existing monitoring VM.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New manifest set or Helm chart deploys:
  - Namespace observability
  - ServiceAccount + ClusterRole granting access to nodes/pods/namespaces (for k8sattributes processor)
  - DaemonSet running otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib (version pinned)
  - ConfigMap with collector config:
    * filelog receiver tailing /var/log/pods/*/*/*.log
    * k8sattributes processor enriching with pod, namespace, container, trace_id
    * OTLP receiver on :4317 (gRPC) and :4318 (HTTP) for in-cluster traces
    * OTLP exporter pushing to monitoring_vm_ip:4317
- Three per-VM OTel Collector containers retired (otel-collector references in compose templates removed)
- Verification: a Spring Boot log line appears in Loki on the monitoring VM with the correct pod/namespace/trace_id labels
- Verification: a trace from Spring Boot or Kong appears in Jaeger on the monitoring VM

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-10
Blocked by: E3-01, E3-03, E3-04, E3-05
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-08: Prometheus kubernetes_sd_configs scrape job + RBAC for monitoring VM
column30:
As an observability engineer, I need the existing external Prometheus to discover and scrape K8s-hosted services so that all metrics keep flowing to the same datastore.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New ServiceAccount prometheus-scraper in namespace observability
- ClusterRole granting get/list/watch on nodes, pods, services, endpoints + nonResourceURLs /metrics
- ClusterRoleBinding to the SA
- SA token extracted, stored as Ansible vault secret on the monitoring VM at /etc/prometheus/k8s-token
- Prometheus role updated with new scrape job using kubernetes_sd_configs:
  - role: pod (with annotation-based filtering)
  - api_server pointing at the k3s host's :6443
  - bearer_token_file pointing to the new secret
  - tls_config insecure_skip_verify true (sprint 2; proper CA in sprint 3)
  - Relabel rules: keep only pods with annotation prometheus.io/scrape: 'true', use prometheus.io/port for port
- Security group on k3s EC2 allows monitoring VM IP on :6443 and :10250
- Verification: Prometheus scrape_targets page on the monitoring VM shows healthy K8s targets

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-09, E3-10
Blocked by: E3-01, SCRUM-63 (security group update)
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-09: Migrate the 17 Grafana alert rules to K8s pod-based label selectors
column30:
As an SRE, I need every Grafana alert rule's selector updated to match the new K8s-hosted target so that alerts continue to fire correctly after migration. This is high-risk: silent failure if missed.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Audit current Grafana alerting configuration: 17 rules in 3 groups (per CLAUDE.md)
- For each rule, rewrite the label selector:
  - Old: instance='application_vm_ip:8080' -> New: pod=~'spring-boot.*', namespace='app'
  - Old: instance='network_vm_ip:8000'     -> New: pod=~'kong.*',        namespace='network'
  - Old: instance='ai_vm_ip:8090'           -> New: pod=~'triage.*',      namespace='ai'
- Updated rules committed to git and applied via Grafana provisioning
- Each rule manually triggered (stop a pod, generate latency, etc.) and verified to fire
- Rule firing test results documented in a checklist
- Triage service receives the test webhook for each rule
- No rule is left with a stale selector

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocks: E3-10
Blocked by: E3-03, E3-04, E3-05, E3-08
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column1: E3-10: End-to-end K8s validation playbook — full pipeline against K8s target
column30:
As an SRE, I need an automated end-to-end test that validates the full pipeline against the K8s-hosted target so that the migration is provably complete.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New playbook playbooks/integration-k8s.yml (or update existing integration.yml)
- Test sequence:
  1. Run load-test.sh against Kong's external endpoint
  2. Verify Spring Boot pod metrics in Prometheus (RED metrics)
  3. Verify Spring Boot logs in Loki, with trace_id label
  4. Verify trace in Jaeger end-to-end (Kong -> Spring -> RDS)
  5. Trigger an alert (e.g. high latency)
  6. Verify Grafana alert rule fires
  7. Verify webhook arrives at triage service
  8. Verify triage service produces a verdict
  9. Verify LLM analysis runs and returns RCA JSON
  10. Verify escalation email is sent (or DISMISS logged)
- All checks scripted, exit code 0 = pass
- Runbook documented in monitoring-docs for manual reproduction
- Coordinates with SCRUM-66 (E1-05) — that story is closed in favor of this one

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: K8s Migration
Blocked by: E3-03, E3-04, E3-05, E3-06, E3-07, E3-08, E3-09
column54: Epic 3 -- Kubernetes Migration of Monitored Target

column30:
Targeted reliability and observability improvements to the AI triage pipeline beyond the MVP. Scope is deliberately narrow: three high-leverage stories covering the most impactful weaknesses identified after Epic 2 (the MVP). Larger AI maturity work (calibration sets, prompt engineering, model A/B testing) is deferred.

Goals:
- Eliminate Ollama-related pipeline hangs (the single biggest reliability issue)
- Replace fragile free-text parsing with strict JSON schema enforcement
- Add self-observability so future iteration is data-driven

Out of scope (future sprint):
- Prompt template versioning + iteration loop
- Calibration set + regression testing for prompts
- Model A/B testing across Ollama variants
- Persistent decision history with database backend
- Few-shot prompting / chain-of-thought
- Per-alert-class prompt variants

column1: AI-01: Ollama call hardening — timeout, retry, circuit breaker, fallback
column30:
As an SRE, I need the triage service's Ollama calls to be resilient to timeouts and failures so that a slow or hung Ollama doesn't stall the entire pipeline.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Configurable per-request timeout on Ollama calls (default 30s, override via env var)
- Retry policy: up to 2 retries with exponential backoff on transient failures (network, 5xx)
- Circuit breaker: after N consecutive failures (default 5) within a window, mark Ollama as 'down' and skip calls for a cooldown period (default 60s)
- Fallback path: when circuit is open or all retries exhausted, the triage service produces a 'NEEDS_HUMAN_REVIEW' verdict with the raw alert context, sent via the existing escalation email path
- Metrics exposed: ollama_request_duration_seconds, ollama_request_total{status}, ollama_circuit_state
- Unit tests for timeout, retry, circuit breaker open/close transitions
- Manual test: kill Ollama mid-request, verify pipeline produces fallback verdict within timeout + retry budget

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI Triage Service Hardening

column1: AI-02: JSON schema enforcement on LLM verdict — Ollama JSON mode + Pydantic validator
column30:
As an SRE, I need the LLM verdict to be structured JSON validated against a strict schema so that the triage service can reliably parse it without fragile string matching.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Ollama API call uses 'format: json' parameter (Ollama JSON mode)
- System prompt instructs the model to return JSON matching a documented schema
- Pydantic model defined for the verdict:
  { verdict: 'ESCALATE'|'DISMISS'|'INCONCLUSIVE',
    confidence: 0-1,
    root_cause: str,
    evidence: [str],
    recommended_actions: [str],
    alert_id: str }
- Validator runs on every LLM response; on schema failure, retry once with the validation error appended to the prompt
- On second failure, escalate to NEEDS_HUMAN_REVIEW (uses fallback path from AI-01)
- Existing free-text 'ESCALATE/DISMISS' parser removed
- Unit tests for valid response, schema failure, retry recovery, double failure
- Schema documented in monitoring-docs

Story Points: 2
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI Triage Service Hardening

column1: AI-03: Triage service self-observability — /metrics with queue depth, latency, error rates
column30:
As an SRE, I need the triage service to expose its own operational metrics so that I can observe and improve its behavior the same way I observe everything else in the platform.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Triage service exposes /metrics endpoint in Prometheus format
- Metrics:
  - triage_alerts_received_total{source}
  - triage_alerts_processed_total{verdict}
  - triage_decision_duration_seconds (histogram)
  - triage_queue_depth (gauge)
  - triage_mcp_request_total{server,status}
  - triage_mcp_duration_seconds{server} (histogram)
  - triage_llm_token_count{type='prompt'|'completion'} (histogram)
  - triage_fallback_total{reason}
  - triage_email_sent_total{status}
- Prometheus scrape annotation set on the triage Service (prometheus.io/scrape='true', prometheus.io/port='8090', prometheus.io/path='/metrics')
- New Grafana dashboard panel 'Triage Service Health' added to unified-overview:
  - Decision throughput (alerts/min)
  - Decision latency p50/p95/p99
  - Verdict breakdown pie chart
  - MCP error rates by server
  - Fallback rate trend
- Dashboard JSON committed to git
- Verification: triggering a test alert produces visible metric updates within 30s

Story Points: 3
Estimate: 0.5 day
Epic: AI Triage Service Hardening
